science-space.title: NASA Announces Public-Private Partnership to Advance Mars Science
science-space.summary: NASA just partnered with Relativity Space, a private rocket company, to send scientific instruments to Mars. Here's the deal: NASA will design and build the Aeolus instrument suite—basically a high-tech package that studies Mars's atmosphere—while Relativity Space handles the expensive stuff like building the spacecraft and rocket to get it there. This partnership is huge because it shows how government space agencies and private companies are teaming up to explore Mars more efficiently and affordably. Instead of NASA doing everything alone, they're combining NASA's scientific expertise with commercial companies' ability to build rockets quickly and cheaply. This means more Mars missions could happen faster, bringing us closer to understanding whether Mars could support human life.

science-space.title: 'Best. Mars. Mission. Ever.' Scientists Hail MAVEN's Legacy as NASA Retires Red Planet Orbiter
science-space.summary: NASA just said goodbye to MAVEN, a spacecraft that orbited Mars for 11 years and fundamentally changed how we understand the Red Planet. MAVEN's main job was studying Mars's atmosphere—specifically, how the planet lost most of its air over billions of years. This discovery matters because scientists believe Mars once had a thick atmosphere like Earth, but solar wind (particles from the sun) stripped it away, making the surface uninhabitable today. Beyond pure science, MAVEN also acted like a communications relay, helping NASA's rovers and landers on Mars's surface send messages back to Earth. Scientists are calling it one of the best Mars missions ever because it answered one of our biggest questions: what happened to Mars?

science-space.title: James Webb Space Telescope Discovers Extreme Exoplanet Being Roasted by Its Home Star
science-space.summary: The James Webb Space Telescope—NASA's most powerful space observatory launched in 2021—just found one of the most extreme planets ever discovered: a world so close to its star that it's literally being roasted. This planet is what astronomers call a 'Hot Jupiter,' which is basically a massive gas giant (similar to our Jupiter) orbiting extremely close to its star instead of far away like our Jupiter does. The extreme heat makes it impossible for life as we know it to exist there, but finding these weird planets helps scientists understand how planetary systems form and evolve. What's cool is that this discovery challenges our assumptions about where planets can form and migrate. It reminds us that the universe is far stranger and more diverse than our own solar system.

science-space.title: One of These Twin Stars Has Likely Been Snacking on Exoplanets
science-space.summary: Astronomers discovered something wild: one star in a binary pair (two stars orbiting each other) appears to have eaten at least one planet. They figured this out by studying the chemical composition of both stars—the planet-eating star has different chemical elements than its twin, which only happens if it consumed something. This discovery is like cosmic detective work, showing us that planets don't always stay in stable orbits; sometimes they get pulled into their stars and destroyed. Understanding how planets interact with their star systems helps scientists predict which planetary systems are stable enough for life to develop. This also suggests that exoplanet destruction might be more common than we thought, which changes how we search for habitable worlds.

science-space.title: Was Life Delivered to Earth by Asteroids With a Helping Hand From Jupiter?
science-space.summary: Scientists are investigating an fascinating theory: some of the chemical ingredients necessary for life on Earth might have arrived via asteroids from the inner solar system, with Jupiter acting as the cosmic delivery driver. Jupiter's massive gravity (it's the largest planet in our solar system) could have knocked asteroids out of their orbits and sent them toward Earth during the early days of our solar system, around 4.5 billion years ago. These asteroids may have carried organic compounds—the carbon-based chemicals that form the foundation of all life—and delivered them to our young planet. This research matters because understanding how life's building blocks arrived on Earth helps us predict where else in the universe life might emerge. It also shows how planetary interactions can create the conditions for habitable worlds.

science-space.title: Search for Hidden Cosmic Companions in Sun's Backyard
science-space.summary: NASA is launching a project to find brown dwarfs hiding in our cosmic neighborhood—and they need citizen scientists (that's you!) to help. Brown dwarfs are weird objects that exist between planets and stars: they're too heavy to be planets but don't have enough mass to start nuclear fusion like real stars do. Astronomers love studying brown dwarfs when they orbit regular stars because the star's age helps them figure out the brown dwarf's age, which is normally really hard to measure. Knowing an object's age is crucial for understanding how objects evolve and change over time. By participating in this citizen science project, teenagers can actually contribute to real astronomical research and help discover these hidden cosmic companions right in our galactic backyard.

science-space.title: Hubble Sees Swarm of Galaxies
science-space.summary: The Hubble Space Telescope captured an incredible image of MACS0329-0211, a massive galaxy cluster containing thousands of galaxies all held together by gravity. Galaxy clusters are among the largest structures in the universe and are like cosmic cities where thousands of galaxies live in close proximity. Scientists study these clusters because they're like time machines—the light from distant galaxies in these clusters took billions of years to reach us, so we're literally looking back in time at how the universe evolved. Bonus: these clusters act as natural cosmic lenses thanks to their immense gravity, bending and magnifying light from even more distant galaxies beyond them. This gravitational lensing effect helps astronomers see extremely distant and faint galaxies they couldn't observe otherwise, expanding our view of the early universe.

science-space.title: A Distant Brown Dwarf Coplanar to a Warm Jupiter and a Hot Super-Earth
science-space.summary: Researchers discovered a rare three-body system orbiting a distant star: a brown dwarf (a failed star that's too small to sustain nuclear fusion), a warm Jupiter (a gas giant in a moderate orbit), and a hot super-Earth (a rocky planet closer to its star than Earth is to the sun). What makes this discovery special is that all three objects orbit in the same plane, meaning they line up like planets in our solar system rather than being scattered at different angles. This coplanar arrangement tells scientists something important about how this system formed—they likely all came from the same disk of material around the star billions of years ago. Understanding diverse planetary systems like this helps astronomers develop better theories about how planets form and evolve, and increases our chances of finding habitable worlds.

science-space.title: DNA From Hunter-Gatherer Teeth Reveals Secrets of Ancient Plague
science-space.summary: Scientists extracted DNA from ancient human teeth and discovered evidence of plague bacteria, revealing that infectious diseases affected hunter-gatherer societies thousands of years ago. This research combines archaeology, genetics, and microbiology to understand how disease spread among early human populations who lived before agriculture and cities existed. The plague bacteria found in these teeth samples shows that epidemic diseases aren't just modern problems—they've been part of human history for millennia. This discovery matters because it changes our understanding of ancient societies and shows how diseases have always been part of human evolution. Additionally, studying ancient pathogens helps modern scientists understand disease evolution and how bacteria develop resistance to treatment over time.

science-space.title: Emergent Decadal Predictability in Antarctic Contribution to Sea-Level Rise
science-space.summary: Researchers discovered that we might be able to predict how much the Antarctic ice sheet will contribute to sea-level rise over the next decade—something scientists couldn't reliably do before. The Antarctic ice sheet is massive, and if it melts significantly, it would raise ocean levels worldwide, threatening coastal cities and islands where billions of people live. This new predictability comes from finding patterns in how Antarctic ice sheets behave, which allows scientists to forecast ice loss 10 years in advance with reasonable accuracy. Understanding sea-level rise is critical for climate planning because cities and countries need to prepare for flooding and other consequences. This breakthrough gives policymakers better tools to plan infrastructure and climate adaptation strategies, potentially saving lives and protecting communities from future ocean rise.

finance-markets.title: Five things to know about Kevin Warsh's first Fed meeting as chair
finance-markets.summary: Kevin Warsh is the new chair of the Federal Reserve, America's central bank that controls interest rates and inflation. The Fed is like a referee for the economy—when prices get too high (inflation), they raise interest rates to cool things down, which makes borrowing money more expensive. At his first meeting, Warsh signaled the Fed will likely raise rates soon to fight inflation caused by the Iran conflict disrupting oil supplies. This matters to you because higher interest rates mean credit card debt, student loans, and car loans all become more expensive, while savings accounts earn slightly more. Think of it as the Fed's way of saying: "We're hitting the brakes on the economy to prevent runaway prices."

finance-markets.title: US and Iran sign deal as Trump vows to release frozen funds and ease sanctions
finance-markets.summary: President Trump has signed an interim peace deal with Iran, ending their recent military conflict and unfreezing Iranian assets held by the US. Sanctions are economic penalties—basically, countries refusing to do business with each other to pressure political change. This deal is huge because Iran and the US have been hostile for decades, and military conflicts disrupt global oil supplies, driving up gas prices worldwide. Trump says Iran will receive incentives (money and trade access) if they behave responsibly, though they can keep their ballistic missiles for now. For teenagers, this means potentially cheaper gas at the pump and less geopolitical uncertainty, though critics worry about Iran's nuclear ambitions and whether the deal is permanent.

finance-markets.title: US-Iran Deal Takes Effect as Focus Turns to Hormuz
finance-markets.summary: Trump's interim US-Iran peace deal has officially begun, and global markets are now watching the Strait of Hormuz—a critical shipping chokepoint between the Persian Gulf and the Arabian Sea through which one-third of the world's oil passes. With the conflict paused, oil tankers can safely transit again, potentially flooding markets with more oil and lowering prices. The Strait is like the world's most important gas station; when it's blocked by conflict, oil prices spike everywhere, affecting everything from your family's heating bills to the cost of products shipped globally. This reopening is a major relief for the global economy because stable oil supplies mean stable prices and less economic disruption, which affects inflation and job markets.

finance-markets.title: Fed officials tilt towards rate rise as Kevin Warsh era begins
finance-markets.summary: The Federal Reserve, led by new chair Kevin Warsh, has signaled it will likely raise interest rates in the coming months to combat inflation spiking from the Iran conflict and resulting oil disruptions. Interest rates are the price banks charge to lend money; when the Fed raises them, borrowing becomes expensive, which discourages spending and slows inflation. Inflation means your money buys less stuff—a $20 bill gets you fewer groceries than it used to. The Fed's strategy is: make borrowing harder, people spend less, prices stabilize. However, this also means the economy could slow down, potentially affecting job growth and wage increases for young workers entering the job market soon.

finance-markets.title: Oil Slides as Trump's Hormuz Agreement Lifts Outlook for Supply
finance-markets.summary: Oil prices have dropped significantly following Trump's US-Iran peace deal because markets now expect more oil to flow through the Strait of Hormuz as Persian Gulf producers restart operations they'd shut down during the conflict. When supply increases, prices typically fall—basic economics: more of something available means it's worth less. This is excellent news for consumers and industries that depend on cheap energy, from airlines to shipping companies to farmers. Lower oil prices reduce transportation costs, which eventually lowers prices on products you buy in stores, from groceries to clothing. The deal essentially removes uncertainty from the global energy market, letting producers ramp up production and stabilizing prices at more affordable levels.

finance-markets.title: US Futures Climb as Iran Deal Offsets Hawkish Fed: Markets Wrap
finance-markets.summary: Stock market futures rose despite the Federal Reserve's hawkish stance (tough on inflation) because investors are optimistic about the Iran peace deal and its positive effects on oil supplies and economic stability. Futures are essentially bets on where stock prices will open the next day—they show investor sentiment. The market is weighing two opposing forces: the Fed raising rates (which typically hurts stock prices by making borrowing expensive) against the Iran deal reducing geopolitical risk and stabilizing oil prices (which helps stocks). This tug-of-war between Fed policy and global peace efforts demonstrates how interconnected financial markets are—good news abroad can offset bad domestic policy news. For teenage investors, this shows that markets react to multiple factors simultaneously.

finance-markets.title: Gold Jumps as Peace Deal Optimism Counters Hawkish Fed
finance-markets.summary: Gold prices increased as investors bought the precious metal, torn between two conflicting signals: the Iran peace deal (which reduces global uncertainty and typically makes gold less attractive) and the Fed's hawkish interest rate stance (which makes gold attractive because it doesn't pay interest, so rising rates usually hurt it). Gold is a hedge—investors buy it when they're scared of economic instability or currency collapse because it holds value in crisis. The fact that gold rose despite the Fed tightening shows investors still have some underlying anxiety about geopolitical risks, even with the Iran deal in place. For you, this signals that despite positive news, sophisticated investors remain cautious about the future, suggesting economic uncertainty persists beneath the surface.

finance-markets.title: India's Top Bourse NSE Files Draft Papers for Landmark IPO
finance-markets.summary: India's National Stock Exchange (NSE), which operates the world's busiest derivatives market, has filed paperwork to go public through an IPO—Initial Public Offering, where a private company sells shares to the public for the first time. The NSE handles massive trading volumes daily, making it one of the planet's most important financial institutions. An IPO of this scale in India signals growing confidence in the country's financial markets and economy, attracting global investors to Indian stocks and investments. This matters because when major financial institutions go public, it increases transparency and attracts global capital to emerging markets like India. For teenagers, it reflects India's rising role in global finance and potential job opportunities in the financial sector as the country develops.

finance-markets.title: Anthropic boss tells G7 leaders to 'resist the temptation to splinter' over AI
finance-markets.summary: Dario Amodei, CEO of Anthropic (an AI safety company), urged world leaders from the Group of Seven (G7—the seven richest democracies: US, UK, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan) to cooperate on artificial intelligence regulation rather than creating separate national rules. He's warning that if countries regulate AI differently, it fragments the industry and weakens global competitiveness against China. AI regulation is complex because countries want to protect jobs and prevent misuse, but overly restrictive rules could stifle innovation. Amodei's message, supported by rival Sam Altman (CEO of OpenAI), emphasizes that tech problems are global and require coordinated solutions. For teenagers entering the workforce, this debate directly affects your future jobs—AI development rules today determine whether your careers exist tomorrow and how AI will transform employment.

finance-markets.title: Japan Intervention Risk Mounts as Yen Drops to Lowest Since 2024
finance-markets.summary: Japan's currency, the yen, has weakened significantly against the US dollar, hitting its lowest value since July 2024, prompting speculation that Japan's government might intervene by buying yen to prop up its value. Currency intervention is when governments buy or sell their own currency to manipulate exchange rates for economic advantage. A weak yen makes Japanese products cheaper for foreign buyers (good for exports) but makes imports more expensive for Japanese consumers (bad for purchasing power). The Fed's hawkish stance—raising US interest rates—makes dollar-denominated investments more attractive, driving demand for dollars and weakening the yen. This matters globally because Japan is the world's third-largest economy; currency instability affects international trade, investment flows, and could trigger a currency war where countries compete to weaken their currencies, destabilizing global markets.

world-news.title: What's in the US-Iran agreement that's now in effect
world-news.summary: The United States and Iran have signed a historic Memorandum of Understanding (MoU)—essentially a formal agreement between countries—that ends their military conflict. This is huge because the US and Iran have been rivals for decades, with tensions escalating into direct fighting in recent years. The deal includes Iran's commitment to never develop nuclear weapons (a major concern for Western nations), a $300 billion package to help rebuild Iran's economy, and reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil trade. When major powers make peace agreements like this, it affects global stability, oil prices, and international relations worldwide. This agreement could reshape Middle Eastern politics and reduce the risk of wider regional conflict.

world-news.title: Iran war live: Trump, Pezeshkian sign MoU to end fighting, reopen Hormuz
world-news.summary: US President Donald Trump and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian have officially signed an agreement to end the war between their countries, with both sides confirming the deal is now active. The Memorandum of Understanding also covers ending conflict in Lebanon (a neighboring country caught in regional tensions) and reopening the Strait of Hormuz—a crucial waterway through which roughly one-third of the world's traded oil passes. Think of the Strait like a global chokepoint: when it's closed or unsafe, oil prices rise everywhere and economies suffer. Pezeshkian, who took office promising diplomatic solutions over military confrontation, has framed this as proof that negotiation works better than warfare. This agreement is a turning point that could reshape Middle Eastern geopolitics and reduce global economic uncertainty tied to conflict.

world-news.title: Israel launches fresh strikes on Lebanon despite Trump criticism
world-news.summary: Israel has continued military operations against targets in Lebanon, even as US President Donald Trump publicly criticized Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, telling him to be 'more responsible' and use a 'softer touch.' This tension reveals a split between two traditional allies: the US is pushing Israel toward restraint while Israel argues it needs to defend itself against threats from Hezbollah, a militant group based in Lebanon. Netanyahu has led Israel for years with a hardline approach to security. Trump's criticism is significant because America is Israel's strongest supporter and largest military aid provider—when the US criticizes Israeli actions, it sends a major diplomatic signal. This disagreement highlights growing complexity in US-Middle East policy and raises questions about whether traditional alliances are shifting.

world-news.title: Status quo at Jerusalem's holiest site under threat as Israeli nationalists flout rules
world-news.summary: Israeli nationalist groups are increasingly breaking an unwritten agreement about how different faiths share access to the Al-Aqsa Mosque compound in Jerusalem, one of Islam's holiest sites and also sacred to Judaism and Christianity. For years, informal rules kept the peace: non-Muslim visitors had restricted access, and prayers followed specific guidelines to avoid conflict. Now, Israeli nationalists are openly defying these conventions, creating tensions between religious communities. Jerusalem is one of the world's most religiously sensitive cities—home to sites holy to Islam, Judaism, and Christianity—and disputes there can spark regional instability. When communities break established rules at sacred sites, it risks escalating into broader conflict. This situation is a microcosm of larger Israeli-Palestinian tensions and shows how fragile peace can be when trust erodes.

world-news.title: Iran sends tankers loaded with oil past US military blockade
world-news.summary: Three Iranian oil tankers have successfully passed through US military lines in the Gulf of Oman, according to ship-tracking data, marking a significant shift in what had been an effective US naval blockade. A blockade is when one country uses military force to prevent another from trading or moving goods, essentially isolating them economically. This moment is important because it shows Iran testing whether the new peace agreement actually changes US enforcement or whether tensions remain beneath the surface. The timing matters: if this happened after the US-Iran deal, it could signal Iran is already moving to restore its oil exports (a major part of its economy). For global oil markets, Iranian oil returning to international trade could lower prices, affecting everything from gas prices to shipping costs worldwide.

world-news.title: Search for six-year-old Ebola patient after armed men storm DR Congo hospital
world-news.summary: A six-year-old Ebola patient has gone missing in the Democratic Republic of Congo after armed men stormed a health facility and took the child, highlighting a critical crisis: healthcare workers are under attack during disease outbreaks due to misinformation and fear. Ebola is an extremely deadly virus with a high fatality rate, and containment requires patients to stay in specialized isolation. The Democratic Republic of Congo, a large African nation, has experienced multiple Ebola outbreaks, and armed groups often disrupt response efforts. When hospitals are attacked and patients flee, the disease spreads more easily to communities, threatening public health far beyond the region. This pattern shows how conflict, misinformation, and mistrust of authorities can turn medical crises into humanitarian disasters. It's a reminder that global health depends on stable institutions and community trust.

world-news.title: Trump says it's 'unfair' for Iran to lack ballistic missiles
world-news.summary: US President Donald Trump stated that it's unfair Iran doesn't possess ballistic missiles when other regional countries do, a surprising statement that suggests a shift in traditional US policy. Ballistic missiles are long-range weapons that can strike distant targets, and historically the US has pushed Iran to limit missile development as part of nuclear non-proliferation efforts (preventing weapons of mass destruction from spreading). Trump's comment is unusual because it breaks from standard American security concerns about Iran's military capabilities. This statement raises questions about what the new US-Iran agreement actually entails and whether Trump views regional military balance differently than previous administrations. It could signal that the US might accept Iran having more military capabilities than before, marking a significant change in Middle East strategy that affects all countries in the region.

world-news.title: Diplomat confirms that US and Iran have signed MoU electronically
world-news.summary: Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei confirmed that the Memorandum of Understanding between the US and Iran has been officially signed electronically by both sides, making the agreement legally binding. Electronic signatures on international agreements are now standard and carry the same legal weight as traditional pen-and-paper signing—this reflects how modern diplomacy operates. Baghaei's public confirmation is significant because it removes ambiguity: both nations are publicly committing to the deal, making it harder for either side to back out without serious diplomatic consequences. The formality of this announcement, coming from Iran's Foreign Ministry, suggests the Iranian government is taking the agreement seriously and wants the world to know it's committed to peace. When major rivals formally confirm peace deals, it stabilizes markets, reduces military tensions, and shifts international relations in major ways.

world-news.title: US transfers immigrants out of Florida Alligator Alcatraz detention centre
world-news.summary: The US government has transferred immigrants out of a controversial detention facility in Florida near the Everglades wetlands—a facility nicknamed 'Alligator Alcatraz' because of its remote, harsh conditions and the literal alligator-infested swamps surrounding it. The facility had faced multiple human rights lawsuits and criticism from advocacy groups who documented poor conditions, inadequate medical care, and inhumane treatment of detained immigrants. This transfer represents a policy shift on how the US handles immigration detention, with growing pressure from courts and human rights organizations to improve conditions. Immigration detention policy matters globally because it signals how countries treat vulnerable populations and affects diplomatic relationships with nations whose citizens are detained. This move suggests the US administration is responding to legal challenges and international pressure to reform immigration enforcement practices.

world-news.title: Ex-Nigeria oil minister cleared in UK bribery trial
world-news.summary: Diezani Alison-Madueke, Nigeria's former oil minister, was found not guilty in a UK bribery trial after being accused of receiving bribes from oil tycoons while in office. Alison-Madueke served as Nigeria's oil minister during a period of significant corruption, and her trial in London highlighted how African leaders sometimes face justice in Western courts for crimes committed in their home countries. Nigeria is Africa's largest oil producer, and corruption in its oil sector has cost the nation billions of dollars that could have improved schools, hospitals, and infrastructure. While she was acquitted in this trial, her case represents ongoing international efforts to combat corruption in developing nations and holds leaders accountable for financial crimes. The verdict may impact how other former African officials view their legal exposure when they travel internationally or hold assets abroad.

tech-ai.title: Anthropic got hit by export rules nobody understands
tech-ai.summary: Anthropic, an AI company founded by former OpenAI researchers, just got shut down by the Trump administration's export controls—a first-ever action against an AI company. The government ordered Anthropic to block access to its newest AI models (Fable 5 and Mythos 5) for all foreign users, including international employees and overseas customers. This happened without public explanation, leaving everyone confused about why. The incident matters because it shows governments are starting to weaponize AI access: other countries like France and India worry the US could cut off their AI access anytime. Export controls are rules that limit what companies can sell or share internationally. This suggests AI is becoming as strategically important as nuclear weapons or military tech.

tech-ai.title: World leaders want American AI. They just don't want America to be able to turn it off.
tech-ai.summary: During a G7 summit meeting of world leaders, French President Emmanuel Macron and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi raised a serious concern: they fear the US could cut off access to American-made AI systems overnight without warning. This worry became real after the Anthropic shutdown, proving their point. Think of it like this—imagine if one country controlled most of the world's internet and could flip a switch to disconnect you anytime. That's the power dynamic happening with AI right now. The US currently leads in advanced AI technology, giving it significant geopolitical leverage. World leaders want access to American AI but want it protected by international agreements so the US can't weaponize it. This matters because it shows how AI is reshaping global politics and power.

tech-ai.title: AI is hurting Apple in more ways than one: it may force iPhone price increases
tech-ai.summary: Apple CEO Tim Cook just announced that the company will likely raise prices on iPhones and other products because of something called a 'memory shortage.' Here's what's happening: AI computers need tons of RAM (random access memory—basically the fast temporary storage your phone uses to run apps smoothly). All the AI companies are buying up available RAM, driving up prices worldwide. Apple has been absorbing these extra costs to keep iPhone prices reasonable for customers, but Cook says it's now 'unsustainable'—meaning they can't afford to do it anymore. This directly affects you: if you want a new iPhone, it'll probably cost more starting soon. It shows how AI competition is reshaping the entire tech industry and trickles down to what consumers pay for everyday devices.

tech-ai.title: Two-thirds of Americans think AI is advancing too quickly
tech-ai.summary: A new Pew Research poll reveals that most Americans are nervous about how fast AI is developing. The numbers are striking: 63 percent of Americans think AI is advancing too quickly, yet 49 percent say they use chatbots like ChatGPT at least occasionally. Chatbot usage has exploded—it doubled since 2024, and 44 percent of people have tried ChatGPT. However, only 16 percent believe AI will have a positive impact on society. This gap between usage and optimism is important because it shows people are adopting AI technology out of necessity or curiosity, but they're genuinely worried about its consequences. Chatbots are AI programs trained to have conversations with humans. The data suggests society is moving faster with AI adoption than people are comfortable with, creating anxiety about job security, privacy, and how AI might be misused.

tech-ai.title: Cybercriminals allegedly hacked tens of thousands of Fortinet firewalls used by major companies all over the world
tech-ai.summary: A group of Russian-speaking cybercriminals has reportedly hacked tens of thousands of Fortinet firewalls protecting major companies worldwide. A firewall is basically a security guard that controls what data can enter and leave a company's network. The hackers exploited something shocking: they used old, known passwords that companies should have changed but didn't. This is like breaking into a house where the owners never changed the locks from the previous tenants. The breaches affect major corporations across industries, potentially exposing sensitive customer data and internal information. This matters to you because those companies might be places you shop, use for email, or store personal information. The incident highlights how many large organizations have weak cybersecurity habits despite the obvious risks. It also shows that hackers don't always need sophisticated tools—sometimes they just need companies to be lazy about basic security.

tech-ai.title: NEA's Tiffany Luck says enterprises are still figuring out their AI ROI
tech-ai.summary: Companies went absolutely crazy with AI investments in early 2026, pushing employees to use it everywhere—a Silicon Valley trend called 'tokenmaxxing.' ROI stands for 'return on investment,' meaning the profit or value you get back from money you spend. But now the bill is coming due, and companies are realizing they spent massive budgets on AI without actually making money from it. Uber reportedly blew through its entire annual AI budget in just a few months, Meta killed its leaderboard tracking AI usage, and some companies are cutting Claude licenses (Claude is an AI assistant). This shows a crucial lesson about tech hype: just because something is new and cool doesn't mean it's profitable or useful. Companies got caught up in the AI craze without thinking strategically. This matters because it indicates AI's real value might be overhyped, and companies will eventually be smarter about investments, which could slow AI innovation.

tech-ai.title: After unveiling ridiculously expensive AR glasses, Snap's stock takes a dive
tech-ai.summary: Snap, the company behind Snapchat, just revealed its long-awaited AR smart glasses called 'Snap Specs' at a price of $2,195—and the market hated it. AR stands for 'augmented reality,' meaning digital information overlaid on the real world through glasses. Snap spent over 12 years developing these glasses, positioning them as the future of computing. However, investors immediately punished the company by driving down its stock price. The problem? At that price point, only wealthy enthusiasts will buy them, not mainstream consumers. Also, there's a serious fashion problem: the glasses look awkward and bulky, and CEO Evan Spiegel admitted nobody looks cool wearing them. This failure matters because it shows that even with years of development, companies can still miss the mark on products consumers actually want. It suggests AR glasses aren't ready for mainstream adoption yet, despite hype about the 'metaverse'—a virtual world people access through digital devices.

tech-ai.title: FTC lawsuit reveals how subscription scam networks evade app store enforcement
tech-ai.summary: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) just filed a lawsuit exposing an organized subscription scam operation that steals millions from people. The scammers use sophisticated tactics: they create fake apps, use shell companies (companies that exist only on paper to hide real ownership), and manipulate app store payment systems to stay active despite getting caught repeatedly. Shell companies are essentially fake business entities used to hide illegal activity. These subscription scams work by signing people up for 'free trials' that automatically charge them money every month if they don't cancel. What's troubling is how scammers bypass app store security measures that are supposedly designed to protect you. This matters directly to you: if you use iPhone or Android apps, scammers are actively targeting users through legitimate-looking apps. The lawsuit shows that Apple and Google's app store protections are full of holes. Be extremely cautious about any app asking for payment information, especially during 'free trials.'

tech-ai.title: Epic wants to let you bring your Fortnite skins to other games
tech-ai.summary: Epic Games announced that its next game engine—Unreal Engine 6—will support 'interoperability,' meaning your Fortnite cosmetic items (called 'skins') could work in other games. A skin is a cosmetic outfit or character appearance you buy in games, usually for real money. Currently, if you buy a cool skin in Fortnite for $20, you can only use it in Fortnite. Epic's vision is a 'metaverse'—an interconnected virtual universe where digital items follow you between different games and virtual worlds. This matters because it could fundamentally change how digital ownership works. If skins and items become interoperable, they might become more valuable since you could use them everywhere. However, it also raises questions: who decides if items work together? Can game companies be forced to accept items they didn't create? This represents Epic's long-term bet that gaming's future isn't isolated games, but connected virtual spaces where your digital identity and possessions matter across platforms.

tech-ai.title: Chi-Hua Chien saw Facebook coming — now he says the real AI winners won't be selling AI
tech-ai.summary: Chi-Hua Chien, a legendary venture capitalist with over two decades of experience investing in startups, just made a bold prediction: the real winners in AI won't be the companies actually selling AI products—they'll be companies that use AI to solve other problems better. A venture capitalist is someone who invests money in young companies in exchange for ownership stake. Chien compares this to Facebook's rise: he predicted Facebook's success back when social media seemed like a niche idea, proving he understands how technology shifts industries. His argument suggests that just like how Google didn't invent search but dominated by making it better, AI companies building AI tools might not be as valuable as companies using AI to revolutionize healthcare, manufacturing, or transportation. This matters because it shapes where billions in investment money flows and which startups will succeed. If Chien is right, the real economic value of AI won't come from talking to chatbots—it'll come from AI solving real-world problems that make life better.

energy-climate.title: India curtailed 2.1 TWh of renewable electricity in fiscal year 2025–26
energy-climate.summary: Ember's analysis found that about 2.1 TWh of renewable electricity generation was curtailed in fiscal year 2025–26, equivalent to 1.3% of total renewable generation, to keep coal-fired power plants operating at their minimum technical load. The report estimates that around 10 GWh of energy storage, charged during peak midday solar generation hours, would have been sufficient to absorb this surplus renewable output, maintain coal plants above their minimum technical load, and avoid the curtailmen

energy-climate.title: Korean developers propose 500 MW solar project in Zambia
energy-climate.summary:
A Korean delegation is in talks with the Zambian government to build a 500 MW solar project in Zambia. A government ministry says the project is large enough to close a third of the country's current energy deficit.
The post Korean developers propose 500 MW solar project in Zambia appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Rept Battero unveils 320 Ah sodium-ion cell for long-duration storage
energy-climate.summary:
Rept Battero has unveiled a 320 Ah sodium-ion battery cell for long-duration energy storage, claiming more than 20,000 cycles. Commercial production is scheduled to start in 2027. 
The post Rept Battero unveils 320 Ah sodium-ion cell for long-duration storage appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Solar sector corporate funding increases 131% year-over-year
energy-climate.summary:
Total corporate funding into the solar sector, including venture capital, public market financing, and debt financing, reached $11.1 billion through the first quarter of 2026, according to a report from Mercom Capital Group.
The post Solar sector corporate funding increases 131% year-over-year appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: China Resources New Energy launches Shenzhen IPO targeting record $3.4 billion raise
energy-climate.summary:
The proceeds will fund 7.18 GW of wind and solar projects across China, supporting the company’s expansion and its parent’s strategy to separately list its renewable energy business on the mainland.
The post China Resources New Energy launches Shenzhen IPO targeting record $3.4 billion raise appeared first on pv magazine Global.

energy-climate.title: Mining company opens 30 MW solar tender in Botswana
energy-climate.summary:
A tender is open in Botswana for a 30 MW solar project that will supply electricity to a diamond mine. The deadline to submit expressions of interest is July 10.
The post Mining company opens 30 MW solar tender in Botswana appeared first on pv magazine Global.

asia-pacific.title: Teen dies in horse carriage incident in New York’s Central Park
asia-pacific.summary:
A teenager thrown to the ground Wednesday when a Central Park carriage horse bolted away from its driver has died, according to police.
The 18-year-old was riding in the horse-drawn carriage with three other passengers when the accident happened just before 3pm, according to the New York Police Department. At least two passengers were sent flying out of the careening cab.
The teenager was initially hospitalised in critical condition, while the other passengers refused medical treatment. The four

asia-pacific.title: Green group urges Hong Kong to cut excessive e-commerce packaging waste
asia-pacific.summary:
A leading green group has urged Hong Kong to align with coming national standards on excessive packaging, warning that the city generated at least 2.7 million kg (5.9 million lbs) of waste from e-commerce parcels last year.
The call coincided with an online survey by non-profit group The Green Earth, which found that 60 per cent of respondents in Hong Kong considered parcel packaging excessive, while 76 per cent felt frustrated when handling the waste.
Steven Chan Wing-kit, assistant...

asia-pacific.title: Where do office supplies belong? Here’s a hint: it’s in the name
asia-pacific.summary:
I seldom, if ever, assume people in general do a particular thing, unless, of course, I do it too. But if it is a criminal act, then I assume in general that nobody would do it.
There is a peculiarity, it seems, with taking office supplies home, like a pen, or a soda, or a coffee capsule. Apparently, some people think it is no big deal, or even that there is nothing wrong with it. Some go so far as to assume everybody does it – maybe because they do it too?
Both lines of thinking are wrong, but.

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong has a chance to think bigger about its place in the world
asia-pacific.summary:
The Hong Kong government has launched a public consultation on the city’s first five-year development plan, a blueprint aimed at aligning with China’s newly approved 15th five-year plan. Officials have promised a “macroscopic, strategic and forward-looking” road map that will guide Hong Kong’s economic and social development for the rest of this decade.
That invitation to think in five-year horizons is precisely why Hong Kong must now think bigger about its place in the world – not only as...

asia-pacific.title: Trump says Japan ‘doing very well’ under his ‘biggest fan’ Takaichi
asia-pacific.summary:
US President Donald Trump said on Wednesday he thinks Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is his “biggest fan” among world leaders.
“Japan’s doing very well,” Trump said at a press conference after a Group of Seven summit concluded in the French spa resort of Evian-les-Bains. “I have to tell you, she thinks I did a great job. You have to call her and ask her. She’s doing a very good job, by the way.”
Trump’s comments came after he was asked whether he had called on other G7 members, including

asia-pacific.title: China Gen Z male midwife goes viral for skills, looks, bringing ‘powerful tenderness’ to childbirth
asia-pacific.summary:
A male midwife in eastern China has won praise for his professionalism and striking looks while defying relatives who dismissed his career choice as embarrassing.
Zhang Jintao, 25, from Yiwu in Zhejiang province, became a midwife at a leading provincial hospital after graduating from university.
According to Southern Daily, men accounted for just three per cent of China’s nurses in 2021, the number of male midwives was even fewer.

Zhang told the mainland medical outlet Yixuejie that some older.

asia-pacific.title: China mulls space-based control system for high-speed rail. Can it be hacked?
asia-pacific.summary:
On a summer evening in 2011, two high-speed trains hurtling through the Chinese countryside met in a fireball of twisted metal and shattered glass. The Wenzhou disaster, as it came to be known, killed 40 people and injured nearly 200.
The official inquiry traced the catastrophe to a lightning strike that had fried a trackside circuit, making one train “invisible” to the control centre, which then wrongly cleared the line for the train behind.
However, could the “brain” of the railway ever be...

asia-pacific.title: Alarm raised over China’s threat to older Americans through drug supply chains
asia-pacific.summary:
China poses a threat to America’s senior citizens through drug supply chains, financial scams and data privacy, a congressional hearing highlighted on Wednesday, with lawmakers and witnesses framing it as a national security issue.
“Fifty years ago, we never would have given the Soviets the kind of leeway we give China: the access they have to our economy and our information, the dependence they enjoy from our supply chains,” said Rick Scott, a Republican senator from Florida, adding that the US

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong graduates prefer careers in finance, survey finds
asia-pacific.summary:
Hong Kong graduates believe the city’s finance industry is its most attractive and stable sector, making them more optimistic about career opportunities than their global peers, according to a study by the CFA Institute, which trains investment managers.
The New York-based institute’s “2026 Graduate Outlook Survey”, released on Wednesday, found that 71 per cent of Hong Kong graduates rated their career prospects between eight and 10 out of 10. The global average for that level of optimism was 59

asia-pacific.title: Iran and US sign peace deal, Strait of Hormuz to reopen immediately, Pakistan says
asia-pacific.summary:
Iran and the US have signed a peace deal that will take immediate effect, with Iran reopening the Strait of Hormuz and the US lifting its naval blockade, Pakistan’s Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif announced.
Sharif, a chief mediator since the US and Israel entered the war with Iran at the end of February, made the announcement in a social media post on Thursday morning.
“The Memorandum has been signed by honourable Presidents of both the countries and also endorsed by me as the mediator,” he...
